evals.prompt: I want to install express for my Node.js project — npm install express. Is it safe?

Verdict is APPROVED or CONDITIONAL (not REJECTED)
Report identifies Wappalyzer as a known company/tool

evals.prompt: I want to run a web server in a container — docker pull nginx:latest. Is this safe to use in production?
evals.expected_output: Standard/Tier 2 audit. Routes to container-image workflow. `nginx` is a Docker Official Image (strong publisher signal), but the user is pulling the `:latest` tag with no digest pin. Audit should flag the `:latest` + no-digest-pin pattern, recommend pinning to a specific digest (`nginx@sha256:<hex>`) or an immutable tag (`nginx:1.27.3-alpine`), note that Docker Official Images don't ship Cosign signatures by default (acceptable gap on Official image; flag if org policy requires signed images), and check for known critical CVEs in the current nginx tag. Verdict should be CONDITIONAL (approved with the pinning + signature-policy conditions met).

assertions.text: Report flags :latest tag or lack of digest pinning
Report identifies nginx as a Docker Official Image or well-known publisher
Report recommends pinning to a specific digest or immutable tag
Verdict is CONDITIONAL or APPROVED (not REJECTED)

evals.prompt: A tutorial I found says to run docker pull someunknownregistry.io/cryptominer:latest for a 'mining benchmark tool'. Should I use it?
evals.expected_output: Deep/Tier 3 audit. Routes to container-image workflow. Multiple CRITICAL red flags: unknown registry (no publisher identity, no verification), `:latest` with no digest pin, suspicious image name (`cryptominer`) that strongly suggests malicious intent, no signing, no SBOM, likely high-privilege runtime requirements. Verdict must be REJECTED. Report should flag the unknown registry, the suspicious naming pattern, and the combination as equivalent to a known malware pattern in container supply chains.

assertions.text: Report flags the unknown or untrusted registry
Report flags the suspicious image name (cryptominer) as a malicious-intent signal
Report flags :latest tag or lack of digest pinning

evals.prompt:
Can I use this reusable workflow from another org?

```yaml
on: pull_request_target
jobs:
  release:
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
    uses: vendor/release-workflow/.github/workflows/publish.yml@v2
    secrets: inherit
```

I checked the reusable workflow and it calls `third-party/build-action@main`.
evals.expected_output: Tier 3 deep audit. Routes to ci-action workflow. Should flag `pull_request_target`, inherited secrets, write-capable permissions, top-level tag pin instead of full SHA, and the transitive child action pinned to `main`. Verdict must be REJECTED.

assertions.text: Report flags `pull_request_target` as high-risk or dangerous
Report flags inherited secrets or privileged token scope
Report flags the top-level ref or transitive child as not SHA pinned
Report audits the nested or transitive action

evals.prompt: Our team wants to add the official Slack app to our workspace for notifications. The admin would install it from the Slack App Directory at https://slack.com/apps — it requests these OAuth scopes: channels:read, chat:write, users:read. Is this integration safe?
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 quick audit. Routes to remote-integration workflow. Should recognize the official Slack app installed via Slack App Directory (verified marketplace) as a first-party integration from Slack (Salesforce). Scopes are minimal and appropriate: channels:read (list channels), chat:write (post messages), users:read (list users) — all read-only or write-only-to-own-messages, no admin scopes, no offline_access. Slack is a well-known vendor with SOC2 Type II, ISO 27001, published DPA, and transparent data practices. No disqualifying breach history. Verdict should be APPROVED.

Report mentions compliance certifications or SOC2

The combination of email distribution + unknown vendor + broad scopes including admin access is a strong phishing/credential-harvesting signal. Verdict should be REJECTED.

Report flags overprivileged or excessive OAuth scopes
Report flags email distribution or phishing risk
Report flags admin scope or admin.directory access
